results.title: The Poppi Soda Lawsuit, Explained
results.author: Bindu Bansinath
results.text:
The Poppi Soda Lawsuit, Explained 

Photo: The Image Party/Shutterstock / The Image Party

Sad news for my fellow Poppi heads: The company behind the viral prebiotic soda alternative is facing a class-action consumer-fraud lawsuit alleging that it misrepresents the beverage’s gut-health benefits. Pour one out for my beloved Raspberry Rose.

Launched in 2020, Poppi combines fruit juice, apple-cider vinegar, and inulin prebiotics — dietary fibers that help beneficial bacteria grow in your bowels, per Healthline. The brand advertises its soda alternative as “gut healthy” and counts celebrities like Kylie Jenner and Post Malone among its legions of fans. The lawsuit, which was filed in a California district court by plaintiff Kristin Cobbs on behalf of herself and “similarly situated” Poppi consumers, states that the company concealed and misrepresented the “true nature” of its products, which may not be so gut healthy after all. Poppi contains two grams of prebiotics per can, which, according to the suit, is too negligible an amount to reap any real benefit. (Poppi’s competitor Olipop, meanwhile, contains nine grams of prebiotic fiber per can.) The suit claims that a consumer would have to down four cans of Poppi for positive gut effects, which would then be offset by the drink’s high sugar content. One nutritionist quoted in the suit calls Poppi “basically sugared water.” The suit further alleges Poppi doesn’t warn consumers about the health risks of its brand of prebiotic, the dietary fiber agave inulin. According to Stanford Medicine, excess inulin can cause liver damage and digestive issues, including bloating, cramps, and diarrhea.

In her suit, Cobbs says the drinks didn’t do much for her gut health and that she was unaware that consuming multiple cans could have negative side effects. Had she known, she says, she would not have purchased the Poppi beverages or “would have paid substantially less for them.” (On the Poppi site, a 12-pack is nearly $30). As an IBS girl, I’m sorry to see it. Diet Coke, anyone?

The Cut has reached out to Poppi for comment. We will update this post when we hear back.

results.title: I Tried Every Flavor of Poppi I Could Find, Ranked From Worst to Best. - Business Insider
results.author: Ted Berg
results.text:
I Tried Every Flavor of Poppi I Could Find, Ranked From Worst to Best. - Business Insider

Review by Ted Berg

I tried nine different flavors of Poppi prebiotic soda. Ted Berg

2024-04-22T12:22:01.537Z

Share

Copy link Email Facebook WhatsApp X LinkedIn Bluesky Threads

Save Saved Read in app

This story is available exclusively to Business Insider subscribers. Become an Insider and start reading now.

- Of all the flavors I tried, the ginger lime was my favorite.
- I was disappointed by a few of the flavors, like watermelon and strawberry-lemon.
- I tried all the flavors of Poppi prebiotic soda I could find and ranked them from worst to best.

It's no secret that prebiotic sodas have grown in popularity over the past couple of years.

In 2023, sales soared for popular brands like Poppi, which gained attention in 2018 after sealing a deal on "Shark Tank."

These carbonated drinks, which contain plant fibers that help good bacteria grow and thrive, claim to support things like gut health and blood-sugar regulation.

Still, I wanted to see if these sodas lived up to the hype. On a recent trip to my local supermarket, I bought all the Poppi prebiotic- soda flavors I could find.

Each 12-ounce can of soda contains under 25 calories, which is significantly less than what you'd find in the same-size serving of Coke or Fanta.

Here's my ranking of the flavors I tried, from worst to best.

## Watermelon was easily my least favorite flavor.

I found that Poppi's watermelon flavor tasted medicinal. Ted Berg

Although it wasn't easy picking my favorite Poppi flavor, I had no trouble selecting my least favorite.

I typically enjoy watermelon-flavored things (it's my go-to Blow Pop flavor, for example), but Poppi's effort at it tasted medicinal to me.

I thought it tasted like the watermelon-flavored toothpaste that hung around in our medicine cabinet for months after my kids rejected it.

## I was disappointed by the strawberry-lemon flavor.

I could make out the distinct tastes of both strawberry and lemon. Ted Berg

Strawberry lemon sounded like a great idea for a soda flavor. When I took a sip, I could taste the distinct flavors of both strawberry and lemon.

However, something about this flavor tasted too artificial to me — especially in the aftertaste. It reminded me of the bubblegum that used to come with packs of baseball cards.

## Raspberry rose was too floral for me.

I didn't love Poppi's raspberry-rose flavor. Ted Berg

I found many of Poppi's most creative flavors to be disappointing, and raspberry rose was no exception.

It had a nice, crisp raspberry flavor, but it was overwhelmed by a perfumy floral taste that became increasingly unpleasant as I drank more.

I wouldn't be surprised if someone handed me something that tasted just like this at the cocktail hour of an outdoor wedding. However, I'd probably only take a few sips before ditching it on an empty table somewhere.

## Poppi's root-beer flavor was fine — but I'd prefer the traditional

results.title: Prebiotic Soda Poppi Is Not as Healthy as You Think, Lawsuit Claims
results.text:
Prebiotic Soda Poppi Is Not as Healthy as You Think, Lawsuit Claims

Skip to Content

%0A%09 %0A " width="60" height="60" alt="Play video"/>

2 min read

If you’re an avid Poppi fan, we’ve got some bad news for you. The company behind the trendy prebiotic soda is facing a class-action lawsuit alleging that the brand has been misleading customers about its health benefits.

According to the brand’s website, Poppi advertises itself as a "healthy" soda combining fruit juice, apple cider vinegar, and inulin prebiotics—dietary fibers that can act as food for healthy bacteria for the gut. The company also includes A-listers like Kylie Jenner, Hailey Bieber, and Post Malone as fans of their products, and that star power has contributed to their beverage’s popularity.

However, the lawsuit, filed Wednesday in a California district court by plaintiff Kristin Cobbs, states that each can of Poppi only contains two grams of prebiotic fibers, which isn’t enough to provide any real health benefits.

Phillip Faraone//Getty Images

“A consumer would need to drink more than four Poppi sodas in a day to realize any potential health benefits from its prebiotic fiber,” the suit says. “However, even if a consumer were to do this, Poppi’s high sugar content would offset most, if not all, of these purported gut health benefits.”

The suit also alleges that Poppi does not warn customers about any health risks that come with consuming agave inulin. Medical studies cited in the document claim that an inulin-based diet “can lead to inflammation and even liver damage at doses as small as 10 to 30 grams per day over a 3-week period.” One nutritionist quoted in the suit goes as far as to say that Poppi is “basically sugared water.”

Since appearing on Shark Tank in 2018, the now-viral Poppi sodas now sell for $2.49 a can, and a 12-pack costs nearly $30. According to the suit, Cobbs was unaware that drinking multiple cans would negatively impact her health. Had she known, she either “would not have purchased the products or would have paid substantially less for them.”

results.title: Poppi Soda Lawsuit Claims Trendy Brand Is 'Basically Sugared Water' - Newsweek
results.author: Monica Sager
results.text:
Poppi Soda Lawsuit Claims Trendy Brand Is 'Basically Sugared Water' - Newsweek

Share

Copy Link

✓ Link copied to clipboard!

Newsweek AI is in beta. Translations may contain inaccuracies—please refer to the original content.

Read original

Speed: 0.5xSpeed: 1xSpeed: 1.5xSpeed: 2x

🎙️ Voice is AI-generated. Inconsistencies may occur.

✖

A class action lawsuit filed against the popular health-conscious soda alternative Poppi accuses the celeb-endorsed brand of false advertising for overstating its gut-health benefits.

Poppi has marketed itself as a healthier alternative to traditional sodas, containing prebiotic fiber along with apple-cider vinegar and fruit juice. But according to a nutritionist cited in the suit, the beverage is "basically sugared water."

Poppi beverages are displayed during the CLD Pre-Festival House 2023. A class action was filed against the group for not being as healthy as claimed. Poppi beverages are displayed during the CLD Pre-Festival House 2023. A class action was filed against the group for not being as healthy as claimed. Phillip Faraone/Getty Images

The complaint, filed in the Northern District of California last week on behalf of a San Francisco woman named Kristin Cobbs, alleges that Poppi only contains two grams of prebiotic fiber, "an amount too low to cause meaningful gut health benefits for the consumer from just one can."

The suit goes on to allege that one would have to drink at least four cans of the soda a day to get any potential gut-health benefits, but they would be offset by the cane sugar contained in the drink.

Cobbs, the plaintiff, is seeking "restitution and all other forms of equitable monetary relief" on behalf of her and other unnamed Poppi drinkers.

Newsweek reached out to the plaintiff's law firm and was told the attorneys were "not interested" in commenting about the suit.

In a statement to Newsweek, Poppi rejected the allegations as baseless.

"We are proud of the Poppi brand and stand behind our products," a spokesperson for the brand said. "We are on a mission to revolutionize soda for the next generation of soda drinkers, and we have diligently innovated to provide a tasting experience that millions of people have come to enjoy. We believe the lawsuit is baseless, and we will vigorously defend against these allegations."

Poppi launched in 2020 after its founders, Allison and Stephen Ellsworth, appeared on the popular ABC show Shark Tank. The brand received a coveted spot on the shelves at Whole Foods, and its sales have since topped $100 million, according to the suit, representing 19% of the U.S. soda market. A 12-pack runs about $24 on Amazon.

The drink is known for its colorful cans, each of which carries the slogan: "Be Gut Happy (and) Be Gut Healthy." Poppi ran an ad during this year's Super Bowl, calling itself "the future of soda."

Instead of the "empty calories and excess sugar," Poppi's drinks combine ingredient with bubbles "without feeling bad about it," according

results.title: The Ultimate Poppi Review: A Healthier Soda
results.text:
-- The Ultimate Poppi Review: A Healthier Soda

top of page

Search

A common New Year’s resolution is the desire to be healthier in one’s daily life. Health trends are very popular on social media as people love sharing and finding little tips and lifestyles that help them to prioritize their health. One of the most popular trends in recent years has been taking foods that a person typically consumes and swapping them out for healthier alternatives that taste similar. From small snacks to full meals, if there’s a food you like, there’s an influencer on social media with a “healthier” version of it. One of the most popular alternatives in recent years has been with soda. Although many well-known soda brands have diet versions of their drinks, many people are still not satisfied with the options presented to them, so they may choose to search for brands that specialize in creating healthier sodas. Poppi is a soda company that creates soda with probiotics and apple cider vinegar. The brand has been on Shark Tank and has been featured by influencers and celebrities, but is it really as good as normal soda? We’re here to find out.

As loyal Whole Foods and Sprouts shoppers, we are no strangers to all sorts of healthy alternatives and probiotic drinks. Seeing the recent rise in Poppi’s popularity, we set out to review these fizzy drinks that promised to be oh-so-good for our gut health. And yes, we looked like maniacs carrying every flavor to the checkout line at the grocery store. It was worth it though, for now we are qualified Poppi experts. However, our stomachs may have actually hurt more than before after drinking this much soda (how ironic!).... Here are our reviews of the fourteen flavors, as well as a review of the “ultimate” Poppi.

Photo provided by authors.

Photo provided by authors.

Strawberry Lemon (7.66/10)

This flavor is absolutely delicious. It tastes just like strawberry lemonade with a fizzy twist and does not taste like a health drink at all. We all drank strawberry lemonade as kids, which makes this Poppi perfect to drink if you’re feeling a bit nostalgic for the classic drink.

Orange (7/10)

Orange was good, but didn’t stand out as great. It tastes just like a Fanta with less bubbles and sugar in it. I would rather just drink a Fanta, honestly.

Raspberry Rose (9/10)

This flavor is definitely the best. First of all, it’s just so cute– the baby pink, smell of rose, and gentle taste of raspberry. It’s also perfectly romantic and makes one happy to be drinking it. As raspberry enthusiasts, we gladly report that the flavor of raspberry itself is spot on, almost reminiscent of real raspberry.

Wild Berry (8/10)

Wild berry is most well-known for being featured in Poppi’s partnership with Post Malone and it is certainly deserving of this spotlight. The only flaw of this drink would be its sweetness, but that also makes it taste more like regular soda. It tastes very similar to a Blue Raspberry Slurpee at the movie theatres; so if
